cataloging_subject.label: Mappula Angliae and Ueber Zeitrechnung
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the composite University of Michigan item CME00149, not a single medieval work. It joins two adjacent prose units from British Library, Harley MS 4011: Osbern Bokenham's Mappula Anglie (ff. 144r–163r) and an anonymous almanac or computistical treatise composed in 1445 (ff. 164r–169r), together with retained portions of Carl Horstmann's German editorial framing. Shared codex placement and Horstmann's report that the almanac is in the later Mappula hand do not establish common authorship. The physical manuscript, Horstmann's two 1887 printed contributions, their journal carrier, the Michigan encoding, and the repository XML remain separate lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Mappula Angliae and Ueber Zeitrechnung
catalog_summary.author: Osbern Bokenham (Mappula Anglie); anonymous compiler (almanac)
catalog_summary.date: Mappula circa 1440; almanac composed 1445; both attested in a manuscript cataloged circa 1450; both printed in 1887, explicitly for the first time in the case of Mappula
catalog_summary.region: Mappula composed at Clare, Suffolk, in East Anglia; almanac composition place unknown; shared witness made in England

notes: A readable composite title supplied from the two retained article headings; it is not asserted as a historical title for one medieval work.
University of Michigan item title; the bracketed conjunction and carrier citation describe the digital item rather than a medieval compilation title.
The title supplied by Bokenham in the epilogue and repeated in the explicit.
Horstmann's printed contribution title, partly retained in the XML.
Horstmann's editorial title; the anonymous medieval text does not supply this German title.

agents.name: Osbern Bokenham
notes: Austin friar, compiler, translator, and adapter of Mappula Anglie. The acrostic and closing Latin couplet identify him; this responsibility does not extend to the adjacent anonymous almanac.

agents.name: Anonymous compiler of the Harley MS 4011 almanac
notes: Cataloging identity for the unnamed author or compiler; common scribal hand with part of the Mappula is not treated as evidence of common authorship.
The chapter-initial acrostic and closing couplet identify Osbern; chapter XV's lost opening initial is reconstructed within that sequence.
Bokenham calls himself the poor compiler and reorganizes the source into fifteen derived chapters plus a prologue and epilogue.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of the England section at the end of Polychronicon Book I

notes: The work condenses, rearranges, omits, and supplements Higden rather than translating word for word.
Compiler describes the assemblage of computistical definitions, calendar calculations, and month-by-month solar tables without asserting whether all material was newly authored.

scope.description: The two Harley MS 4011 texts printed on pp. 6–41

date_statements.display: Circa 1440; in the 1440s and before the adjacent almanac's 1445 calculation
notes: Horstmann proposes circa 1440; Laferrière places the translation in the 1440s. No false exact normalized interval is supplied for circa dating.
The internal calculation says 1,444 years had elapsed by the preceding Christmas, which supports composition in 1445.
This dates the surviving witness, not necessarily the composition of both abstract works.
Horstmann explicitly describes Mappula as printed here for the first time; the contribution begins on p. 1 and its medieval text on p. 6.

The separately headed almanac contribution begins on p. 34. Horstmann does not explicitly claim publication priority for it, and no earlier printing was identified in the inspected sources; 1887 is therefore recorded as the first verified publication rather than an unqualified first-ever publication.
Horstmann identifies a Suffolk base altered by two scribal hands; this is not transferred to the anonymous almanac.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Latin couplets, quotations, source phrases, and computistical terminology

notes: Latin is present although the legacy XML TEXT/@LANG value lists only enm and ger.

form_statements.description: Both medieval works and the retained editorial framing are prose; twenty-three encoded Latin verse lines are embedded quotations or closing material rather than a change in the works' predominant form.

scope.description: Horstmann's edited and annotated 1887 presentation as selectively encoded by Michigan

subject_statements.label: English regions, islands, roads, rivers, shires, and cities
English laws, kingdoms, bishoprics, peoples, languages, and customs
Marvels, natural resources, and wondrous places of England
Solar year, solstices, equinoxes, daylight, and calendar drift

summaries.value: Mappula Anglie is Osbern Bokenham's Middle English geographical companion to an English saints' book, translating and adapting the final England chapters of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon Book I into a seventeen-chapter prose treatise on the names, dimensions, resources, marvels, regions, roads, rivers, cities, shires, laws, kingdoms, bishoprics, peoples, languages, and customs of England. The adjacent anonymous 1445 almanac defines divisions of time, explains daily and annual cycles, solstices and equinoxes, and gives two month-by-month sequences for daylight and the lengthening or shortening of days. Horstmann printed both from Harley MS 4011 in 1887, explicitly describing Mappula as appearing in print for the first time; 1887 is the first verified publication of the almanac in the inspected sources.

The XML contains two DIV1 elements and forty-one DIV2 elements. The first DIV1 has seventeen numbered chapters, including prologue and epilogue; its final epilogue is structurally mislabeled N=2 although the heading correctly says chapter XVII. The second DIV1 has introductory computistical prose followed by twenty-four month divisions: a January–December daylight/twilight sequence and a second January–December sequence tracking the year's lengthening and shortening days. Only the first sequence has month headings in the XML.

CME00149 is not a continuous encoding of Horstmann's Mappula contribution. It retains the contribution title and manuscript/source lines from printed p. 1, omits the German introduction on pp. 1–5, then retains the abbreviation note and medieval text on pp. 6–34. The almanac is retained completely on pp. 34–41. Printed p. 34 is shared by the end of Mappula and the beginning of the almanac; p. 41 ends the almanac and begins the next journal article.

extent.description: Two prose works in two DIV1 elements: seventeen Mappula chapter divisions and twenty-four almanac month divisions. The Mappula contains two LG elements and three additional isolated L elements, totaling twenty-three embedded Latin verse lines; these do not change the work-level prose classification. The encoding has 53 P, 209 NOTE, and 36 PB elements spanning printed pp. 6–41, with the almanac heading beginning on printed p. 34 before its first PB 35.

content_parts.label: Retained Horstmann editorial framing and apparatus
content_parts.summary: German contribution headings, source and manuscript lines, the Mappula abbreviation-expansion statement, editorial footnotes, and signed closers retained around the two medieval texts. Horstmann's substantial Mappula introduction on printed pp. 1–5 is omitted except for the relocated heading and source lines.

locators.value: heading/source line p. 1; retained abbreviation note p. 6; notes and closer through p. 34
heading/headnote p. 34; notes and closer through p. 41
DIV1 heads, HEADNOTE, NOTE, DATELINE, SIGNED, CLOSER, and TRAILER elements

notes: Modern editorial framing is not counted as a third medieval work.

content_parts.label: Mappula Anglie
content_parts.summary: Bokenham's seventeen-chapter prose geography of England: a prologue explaining its purpose as a companion to his English saints' book, fifteen chapters translated and adapted from the final England section of Polychronicon Book I, and an epilogue naming the work, describing his compiler-translator role, and completing the authorial acrostic.

locators.value: title/source line p. 1; medieval text and abbreviation note pp. 6–34

notes: Chapter XV begins acephalously in Horstmann's transcription because he reports a lost leaf; the current physical state cannot be checked while images are unavailable.
The epilogue is DIV2 N=2 in the XML, a structural typo contradicted by its own chapter XVII heading and the seventeen-item contents list.

content_parts.label: Anonymous Harley MS 4011 almanac
content_parts.summary: An anonymous prose computistical treatise composed in 1445. It defines divisions and ages of time, describes parts of the day, explains solstices, equinoxes, and accumulated calendar drift, and supplies two annual month sequences for daylight/twilight and the lengthening or shortening of days.

locators.value: DIV1[2]; begins on printed p. 34 before PB 35; PB 35–41; 24 DIV2 month units

notes: The British Library supplies the title An Almanac; Ueber Zeitrechnung is Horstmann's editorial title.
The same hand as the later Mappula section is evidence about scribal production, not authorship.

related_works.title: Polychronicon, Book I, final England chapters
notes: Bokenham says he drew fifteen chapters into English from the end of Ranulf Higden's first book. Horstmann identifies the final twenty-two Latin chapters, condensed and adaptively rearranged; no physical exemplar is identified.

related_works.title: The Abbotsford English legendary attributed to Osbern Bokenham
notes: The Mappula prologue presents the geography as a reference aid for English places mentioned in an earlier saints' book. Horstmann could not identify it; Laferrière summarizes Simon Horobin's 2008 argument that the Abbotsford legendary is Bokenham's referenced compilation. This attributed identification must not be silently equated with Bokenham's separate extant female saints' legends.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML CME00149, pinned Kyvero-Press/CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; IDG; TEXT; BODY; DIV1[1–2]; PB 6–41
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for identifiers, retained headings and editorial matter, both medieval texts, work self-description, internal 1445 computation, encoded structure, language, form, and page markers.

evidence.citation: Local element-count and hierarchy inspection of CME00149.xml against the pinned repository file.
evidence.summary: Confirms two DIV1 units, 41 DIV2 units, 53 P, 209 NOTE, 36 PB, 2 LG, and 23 L elements; maps Mappula to PB 6–34 and the almanac heading at printed p. 34 followed by PB 35–41; identifies DIV2 N=2 on the chapter XVII epilogue.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ‘Mappula Angliae, von Osbern Bokenham’ and ‘Ueber Zeitrechnung,’ Englische Studien 10 (Heilbronn: Gebrüder Henninger, 1887), 1–41.
evidence.locator: volume title PDF p. 7; Mappula contribution printed pp. 1–34 / PDF pp. 19–52; medieval text begins printed p. 6 / PDF p. 24; almanac printed pp. 34–41 / PDF pp. 52–59
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for carrier title and date, article boundaries, manuscript shelfmark and loci, Bokenham authorship and source analysis, circa-1440 dating and Suffolk dialect proposal, scribal-hand observations, editorial abbreviation policy, complete printed texts, and the almanac's 1445 date.
notes: The internally inspected 1887 Heilbronn title page controls over Internet Archive's generic serial metadata, which misleadingly reports 1877 and Leipzig for this item.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Harley MS 4011, English miscellany.
evidence.locator: Mappula ff. 144r–163r; An Almanac ff. 164r–169r; whole-codex description
evidence.summary: Authoritative current evidence for shelfmark, exact component loci, supplied almanac title, English origin, 1445–1455 date range, paper support, dimensions, extent, named scribes, binding, physical access route, and current statement that digital images are unavailable.

evidence.citation: Anik Laferrière, The Austin Friars in Pre-Reformation English Society (DPhil thesis, University of Oxford, 2017).
evidence.locator: thesis p. 39 / PDF p. 54; thesis pp. 57–58 / PDF pp. 72–73; thesis pp. 68–70 / PDF pp. 83–85
evidence.summary: Supports Mappula's Polychronicon basis, composition at Clare, placement of the translation in the 1440s, name acrostic, and the current attributed identification of Bokenham's referenced English legendary with the Abbotsford manuscript.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00149 item record.
evidence.summary: Supports the public item title, mixed inherited author heading, carrier citation, stable identifier, public digital-text access, and the provider's Public Domain / No Copyright–United States statement.
notes: The item-level author heading joins Bokenham and Horstmann but does not make Horstmann a medieval coauthor or extend Bokenham's authorship to the anonymous almanac.

open_questions.question: Which Polychronicon exemplar, recension, and any additional written or oral sources underlie Bokenham's omissions, rearrangements, and additions?
next_steps: Collate Mappula chapter by chapter against critical editions and manuscript recensions of Polychronicon Book I.
Separate demonstrable direct source dependence from common encyclopedic authorities and Bokenham's local Clare material.

open_questions.question: Who compiled the 1445 almanac, where was it composed, and what computistical sources or tables were used?
next_steps: Compare the definitions, calendar-drift calculation, and two monthly sequences against computus and almanac traditions.
Inspect the manuscript hand and surrounding Harley MS 4011 contents without using scribal identity as automatic proof of authorship.

open_questions.question: How does Horstmann's reported lost leaf at the beginning of Mappula chapter XV correspond to the manuscript's current mounting, foliation, and physical state?
next_steps: Obtain whole-codex images or repository clarification and inspect ff. 159–163 and neighboring guards.
Reconcile the absent H acrostic initial, textual lacuna, historical foliation, and current catalog description.

open_questions.question: How securely does the Abbotsford English legendary correspond to the saints' book referenced in Mappula's prologue, and what is its exact textual relationship to Bokenham's other legends?
next_steps: Consult Simon Horobin's 2008 argument directly and inspect the Abbotsford manuscript's contents, lacunae, language, and authorship evidence.
Keep the attributed legendary distinct from Bokenham's female saints' legends and from the Mappula itself.

open_questions.question: Can a complete, reusable color facsimile of Harley MS 4011 be obtained and mapped precisely to both works?
next_steps: Request or order the complete physical object, including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and every recto and verso where handling permits, rather than only ff. 144r–169r.
Record the supplied sequence and map Mappula ff. 144r–163r and the almanac ff. 164r–169r within it.
Obtain written reuse terms before repository redistribution.

notes: The XML revision date 8-24-16 is encoding-maintenance metadata, not a composition, manuscript, or publication date.

The legacy TEXT/@LANG value enm ger omits embedded Latin and uses ger rather than the modern bibliographic code deu; normalized language statements preserve the observed layers without rewriting the source.
No public manuscript facsimile was verified on 2026-07-13. That negative finding is date-bounded and does not exclude private, paid, or unindexed reproductions.
Horstmann's Berlin, August 1885 signature dates editorial preparation of the Mappula contribution, not medieval composition or the 1887 volume publication.
